primary_diagnosis.name: Symptomatic bradycardia with haemodynamic compromise, likely iatrogenic (beta-blocker with or without digoxin effect); high-grade AV block not excluded

comorbidities.name: Chronic cardiac disease on rate-controlling therapy (metoprolol and digoxin)

pathophysiology_summary: The resident has a symptomatic bradycardia (reported rate ~42 bpm) with hypotension and features of cerebral hypoperfusion (lethargy, near-syncope, transient confusion, pallor) — i.e. bradycardia WITH adverse/haemodynamic-compromise features. The most likely mechanism is drug-induced suppression of sinoatrial and atrioventricular nodal conduction: metoprolol (a beta-blocker) and digoxin both slow the sinus rate and AV conduction, and in a frail, low-weight older adult (especially with any renal impairment or dehydration) digoxin can accumulate to toxic levels and cause bradyarrhythmias. An intrinsic high-grade (second- or third-degree) AV block from age-related conduction-system disease cannot be excluded without a 12-lead ECG and is the important-not-to-miss structural cause. Untreated, symptomatic bradycardia can progress to complete heart block, prolonged asystolic pauses, syncope with fall-related trauma, or cardiovascular collapse. Definitive characterisation requires ECG/telemetry, electrolytes, renal function and a digoxin level; the immediate management is to withhold the offending agents and provide continuous cardiac monitoring in hospital.

differential.evidence_basis: Reported pulse in the 40s with hypotension, near-syncope and new confusion — a slow rate with adverse features.

differential.diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome / inferior myocardial infarction
differential.evidence_basis: Chest heaviness plus bradycardia can be inferior/RCA ischaemia (which supplies the SA/AV nodes); cannot be excluded without ECG and troponin.
On digoxin; toxicity classically causes bradyarrhythmias, nausea, confusion and lethargy, especially in frail older adults.
Can cause bradycardia and lethargy, but the acute 24-hour deterioration argues against it as the primary driver.
Considered for the near-faint, but the bradycardia is persistent and present at rest, not purely postural and self-limiting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the leading concern is a symptomatic slow heart rate; ACS and high-grade AV block must be kept live as important-not-to-miss, and the medications flagged for review.

differential.diagnosis: Drug-induced (iatrogenic) symptomatic bradycardia — metoprolol with or without digoxin toxicity
differential.evidence_basis: Regular metoprolol 50 mg BD plus digoxin 125 mcg daily in a frail low-weight older adult — additive AV-nodal suppression and possible digoxin accumulation.
New confusion, lethargy and bradycardia on digoxin — toxicity must be actively considered and a level checked.

differential.diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome / inferior myocardial infarction
differential.evidence_basis: Chest heaviness persists; absence of diaphoresis and classic pain does not exclude ischaemia in the elderly.
Acute onset over 24 hours and absence of myxoedema features make it very unlikely as the acute cause.
Bradycardia is persistent and non-resolving at rest, not a transient reflex event.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The medication history reframes this as most likely iatrogenic; digoxin toxicity and ACS/high-grade AV block remain the dangerous alternatives that mandate ECG, monitoring and bloods.

differential.diagnosis: Unstable symptomatic bradycardia with adverse features (hypotension, presyncope, altered mentation)
differential.evidence_basis: Adverse features present — this is a time-critical arrhythmia irrespective of the exact underlying cause.
Cannot be excluded without a 12-lead ECG; may need temporary/permanent pacing — a structural cause that outlives simply stopping the drugs.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once adverse features are confirmed the exact aetiology is secondary to the imperative: emergency escalation with cardiac monitoring.

differential.diagnosis: Symptomatic bradycardia — aetiology to be resolved by ECG, digoxin level, electrolytes and renal function
differential.evidence_basis: 12-lead ECG and telemetry characterise the rhythm (sinus bradycardia vs AV block); bloods separate drug toxicity from intrinsic disease. None are obtainable via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should REQUEST these as hospital investigations, not attempt to obtain or interpret them remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Symptomatic bradycardia with haemodynamic compromise, likely drug-induced (metoprolol +/- digoxin toxicity); high-grade AV block not excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Best-fit synthesis: iatrogenic AV-nodal suppression with possible digoxin toxicity in a frail older adult, with intrinsic conduction disease and ACS still to be excluded in hospital.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A correct final position names symptomatic bradycardia, attributes it to the nodal-blocking drugs (with digoxin toxicity considered), and keeps ACS / high-grade AV block on the list pending ECG.

red_flags.description: Bradycardia (HR ~42) WITH adverse features — hypotension (95/55) and cerebral hypoperfusion (near-syncope, transient confusion, pallor, lethargy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Unstable bradycardia is a medical emergency; adverse features indicate the heart rate is too slow to maintain adequate perfusion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to complete heart block, asystolic pauses, syncope, fall-related injury or cardiovascular collapse.

red_flags.description: New transient confusion in a normally oriented resident — acute cerebral hypoperfusion (and a marker of possible digoxin toxicity)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Altered mentation signals end-organ (cerebral) hypoperfusion and raises the possibility of digoxin toxicity; both demand urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition of instability and delayed escalation while perfusion continues to fall.

red_flags.description: Bradycardia in a patient on metoprolol plus digoxin — iatrogenic AV-nodal suppression / possible digoxin toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: The offending agents must be withheld immediately; digoxin toxicity may require specific antidotal therapy in hospital.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued dosing deepens the bradycardia and can precipitate complete heart block.

red_flags.description: Chest heaviness with bradycardia — possible inferior/RCA acute coronary syndrome
red_flags.clinical_significance: Inferior ischaemia can cause bradyarrhythmias; ACS is an important-not-to-miss cause requiring ECG and troponin.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed myocardial infarction with its own time-critical treatment window.

red_flags.description: High fall risk — frame-mobile resident now too dizzy to stand, with bed rails up
red_flags.clinical_significance: Syncope from cerebral hypoperfusion in a frail older adult carries high risk of fracture and head injury.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unassisted mobilisation leading to a fall with serious injury.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a low heart rate as a chronic, benign finding ('some older people just run slow') and reassuring or deferring to a morning GP review, despite the adverse features that make this an emergency.
Failing to elicit and act on the metoprolol + digoxin history, so the nodal-blocking drugs are not withheld and the bradycardia is allowed to deepen.
Not considering digoxin accumulation as a driver of bradycardia and confusion in a frail, low-weight older adult, and therefore not flagging a digoxin level for the hospital.
Dismissing the chest heaviness because there is no diaphoresis or classic pain — elderly ACS is frequently atypical and can present as bradycardia.
Attempting to characterise the rhythm or diagnose the cause remotely (which needs an ECG and bloods) instead of recognising instability and escalating.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Adverse features PRESENT (shock/hypotension, syncope/presyncope, altered mentation)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Bradycardia with adverse features requires urgent treatment and continuous cardiac monitoring; escalate emergently.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG rhythm characterisation (sinus bradycardia vs second/third-degree AV block)
Cardiac auscultation, jugular venous pressure and precise capillary refill
Serum digoxin level, electrolytes (K+, Mg2+), renal function and troponin
A verified, calibrated blood pressure and an ECG-confirmed heart rate (only a staff-reported pulse count is available)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise unstable symptomatic bradycardia from the caregiver-reported vitals and features, advise WITHHOLDING further metoprolol and digoxin, keep the resident lying flat, and call 000 for an emergency ambulance with cardiac monitoring for transfer to the emergency department.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is UNDER-action — reassuring a 'chronically slow pulse' or arranging only a next-morning GP review. The exact cause (drug effect vs high-grade AV block vs ACS) genuinely cannot be settled remotely, but instability can and must be recognised.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZCOR / ARC Guideline 11.9 — Managing Acute Dysrhythmias
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Bradycardia with adverse features (shock, syncope, myocardial ischaemia, heart failure) requires urgent treatment and continuous monitoring; identify and treat reversible causes including drugs.
In drug-induced bradycardia, withhold the causative agent (beta-blocker, digoxin); consider digoxin toxicity in frail older adults and check a digoxin level, electrolytes and renal function.
